title: #eng-releases: 1741083310
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Hey folks — looking for Product/CS eyes on the proposed Runtime LTS release notes taxonomy before we bake it into the generator. What would you *expect* to see as an enterprise reader vs what’s noise?

Connor O'Brien: Current draft sections: Summary, Security fixes (CVE), Critical fixes, Other fixes, Perf notes, Upgrade notes, Compatibility matrix, Known issues/workarounds, Artifact verification.

Elliot Price: Link to draft template in Confluence (WIP): /eng-serving-runtime/release-notes/runtime-lts-release-notes-format-and-taxonomy :point_up:

Marissa Cole: Skimmed — structure is solid. Main feedback: we need a VERY explicit “What changed for me?” at the top.
Marissa Cole: For enterprise folks reading at 2am: 1) should I upgrade 2) what will break 3) how do I roll back.
Marissa Cole: Suggest a top banner like:
- Impact: Optional / Recommended / Mandatory
- Affected customers: Hosted/Dedicated/Private
- Time to apply + any required restarts

Paula Mendes: +1. Also add “Customer-visible behavior changes” as a first-class subsection (even if empty most patches).
Paula Mendes: Right now the “Other fixes” bucket could hide behavior changes (e.g., batching heuristic tweaks). We should force it to be called out.

Rachel Kim: From CS side: Compatibility matrix is a must, but it needs to be *actionable*.
Rachel Kim: Like: Runtime 1.18.1-lts compatible with:
- Private installer >= X
- Helm chart >= Y
- NVIDIA driver >= Z (if relevant)
- Supported GPU SKUs list
Rachel Kim: Otherwise we get a bunch of “does this work with our stack?” tickets.

Jasmine Liu: Also please include “Known issues introduced in this patch” vs “Known issues still present”. Customers read “Known issues” and assume it’s all old.
Jasmine Liu: Even a line like “No new known issues in this patch” is reassuring.

Dev Patel: Support asks:
1) Put release date + timezone at the top (we copy/paste into Zendesk macros)
2) Add “How to get help” / escalation pointer (support channel + what data to include)
3) In known issues, include symptom + quick mitigation + whether backport planned + target patch if known
Dev Patel: This reduces ping-pong.

Soojin Lee: Marketing-ish but still important: define LTS terms inline once.
Soojin Lee: Enterprise buyers are picky about what “LTS” means. Recommend short box: support window, patch cadence, backport policy headline (“security + Sev1/Sev2 only”), no feature promises.
Soojin Lee: Helps Sales/CS forward the notes without extra context.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if we say “Mandatory” we need criteria. Otherwise it becomes political.
Sean Gallagher: Could be “Mandatory if: security critical, Sev1 crash/data corruption, SLO impact observed in prod, or active exploitation.”
Sean Gallagher: And include “Operational risk” callout: e.g., “This patch changes scheduler queueing behavior under overload.”

Allison Grant: On perf notes: please include the exact benchmark set + deltas + noise caveats.
Allison Grant: Something like:
- Workloads: chat-8k, summarization-2k, mixed-lens
- GPUs: A100-80G, H100
- Metrics: p50/p95 latency, tok/s
- Result: within +/- X% vs baseline
Allison Grant: Even if summarized, a link to the perf-canary artifact is gold.

Marissa Cole: Another thing: enterprise customers ask “what am I pinned to?”
Marissa Cole: Can the release notes include the *Git commit* / tag + artifact digests? (Even if the full verification steps are elsewhere.)

Elliot Price: Yep, template has “Release identifiers” but we can make it more prominent:
- git tag
- commit SHA
- container image tags
- sha256 checksums
- SBOM link (when available)

Quentin Harris: Re: compatibility matrix — we should keep it short but consistent. Proposed fields:
- Runtime version
- API compatibility (same as mainline 1.18)
- Model compat notes (if any)
- Private installer min
- Helm chart min
- CUDA/driver range (only when it changes)
Quentin Harris: I can own keeping that matrix accurate per patch.

Connor O'Brien: This is super helpful. A couple follow-ups:
1) Do we want separate “Dedicated ops notes” vs “Private ops notes”?
2) How detailed should “Other fixes” be? We’re trying to stay slower-change.

Rachel Kim: Separate ops notes: yes, but keep it minimal.
Rachel Kim: Private readers need steps (upgrade sequencing + rollback). Dedicated readers mostly need “no customer action” vs “requires rolling restart” and “expected impact window.”

Dev Patel: For “Other fixes” — as long as you label customer impact, it’s fine.
Dev Patel: We don’t need every internal refactor. But if it changes logs, metrics names, default flags, or failure modes, we need it.

Paula Mendes: Maybe enforce a rule: every line item has a “Customer impact: none / low / medium / high” tag.
Paula Mendes: If we can’t say impact, we shouldn’t ship it into LTS.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: to impact tags. Also add “Monitoring changes” if any dashboards/alerts need updates.

Allison Grant: +1. If a metric name changes, it’s not “Other.” That’s an ops change.

Soojin Lee: Wording suggestion: avoid “bugfixes” as a bucket. Use “Fixes” + “Known limitations.” Lawyers get nervous about “bug free” vibes.

Marissa Cole: Also: people want to know what *didn’t* change.
Marissa Cole: For LTS, a short “Stability statement” helps: “No kernel autotune default changes” / “No scheduler policy changes” unless otherwise noted.

Connor O'Brien: Love that. We can add a “Stability / invariants” block:
- Kernel autotune defaults: unchanged
- Scheduling policies: unchanged
- Default batching heuristics: unchanged
(And if we deviate, it’s called out.)

Elliot Price: Action items I’m capturing:
- Add top banner: Impact + audience + restart requirements
- Split known issues into new vs existing
- Add explicit customer-visible behavior changes section
- Make identifiers (tag/SHA/digests) prominent
- Perf notes: structured summary + link to perf-canary artifact
- Add escalation/support pointer + required diagnostics
- Add stability/invariants block

Connor O'Brien: Great — I’ll update the template + feed these requirements into ENG-4826 (release notes generator LTS mode). Thanks all. :pray:

Rachel Kim: When you update, tag me — I’ll sanity check from “what will my customer ask next?” perspective.

Dev Patel: Same, happy to review final version + draft a Support macro based on the top banner.

Marissa Cole: :+1: Once it’s stable we’ll align on how Console links/render it (PM-914).

title: #eng-releases: 1741045566
preview:
Connor O'Brien: seeing a weird one: release branch CI failing but main is green. Branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` (cut yesterday). Failing job: `release-ci / build-runtime-images`.

Connor O'Brien: link: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/actions/runs/9142218832

CI Bot: Workflow run failed: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Sahana Iyer: pulled logs. it’s dying during toolchain bootstrap + then again when trying to upload artifacts.

Sahana Iyer: excerpt:
```
==> Step: Setup toolchain
+ ./ci/install_toolchain.sh
Detected branch: runtime-1.18-lts
Using toolchain manifest: ci/manifests/toolchain-release.yaml
Installing clang-17...
E: Unable to locate package clang-17

==> Step: Build
ninja: error: loading 'build.ninja': No such file or directory

==> Step: Publish checksums
+ ./ci/publish_checksums.sh
remote: Permission to redwood/runtime-artifacts denied to github-actions[bot].
fatal: unable to access 'https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts/': The requested URL returned error: 403
```

Devon Price: two separate issues then: runner image doesn’t have apt repo for clang-17, AND token perms for publish step.

Sean Gallagher: why is it only on release branch? main uses the same workflow right?

Sahana Iyer: not exactly. main uses `toolchain-main.yaml` + self-hosted runner group `perf-ci-large`. release branches use `ubuntu-22.04` hosted runners + `toolchain-release.yaml` to keep it “stable”.

Connor O'Brien: ok that explains clang-17 missing. release manifest probably points at 17 but runner’s apt sources don’t have llvm repo configured.

Kira Volkov: also the 403: on release branches we tightened default permissions last week (contents: read). publish script pushes to `runtime-artifacts` repo, so it needs a PAT or workflow permission with `contents: write` + access to that repo.

Rafael Mendes: (reaction: :eyes:) do we have secrets available on release branches? sometimes GH blocks org secrets on non-default branches depending on env rules.

Sahana Iyer: yep, looks like `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` is empty in that run. log line:
```
WARNING: RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN not set; falling back to GITHUB_TOKEN
```
then 403.

Devon Price: I think the secret is scoped to `main` environment only. release workflow uses `environment: release` but the env doesn’t include that secret.

Connor O'Brien: so fix should be:
1) pin toolchain install to our prebuilt toolchain container (no apt)
2) move artifact publish to use `release` environment with the PAT + allow it on release branches

Allison Grant: quick check: this is blocking perf gate artifacts too? if publish fails we won’t get perf-canary summary attached.

Sahana Iyer: yep, `publish_checksums.sh` is in the same job right before it uploads the build manifest + checksums.

Sean Gallagher: for immediate unblock, can we rerun with manual override / skip publish?

Connor O'Brien: would rather not skip; enterprises will expect checksums/provenance even for RCs. but we can get a green build first to validate branch.

Devon Price: I can push a workflow hotfix: add llvm apt repo step for release branches as a short-term patch, then follow up w/ container pin.

Sahana Iyer: +1 short term. add:
```
- name: Add LLVM apt repo
  run: |
    wget -O - https://apt.llvm.org/llvm-snapshot.gpg.key | sudo apt-key add -
    sudo add-apt-repository "deb http://apt.llvm.org/jammy/ llvm-toolchain-jammy-17 main"
    sudo apt-get update
```
then `apt-get install clang-17` should work.

Kira Volkov: re token: we should not rely on `GITHUB_TOKEN` cross-repo. safest is to use `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` PAT with least privilege on that repo.

Rafael Mendes: can we confirm why secret not available? GH settings: env `release` requires approval? could be pending.

Sahana Iyer: checked run summary: no “Waiting for approval” banner. It’s just not present. likely never added to `release` env.

Connor O'Brien: @Sahana can you add the token to `release` environment + restrict to `runtime-*-lts` branches?

Sahana Iyer: I don’t have admin perms for env secrets. we need someone from Infra or repo admin.

Devon Price: I can ping Infra (INT-3302 was about release branch secrets, might still be open). @Rafael can you help?

Rafael Mendes: yep, I can update env secrets. what’s the secret name exactly?

Connor O'Brien: `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` (PAT for https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts). Also `COSIGN_KEY_RELEASE` later but not in this job yet.

Rafael Mendes: ok.

CI Bot: Re-run requested by Connor O'Brien (attempt #2)

Devon Price: I pushed a tiny workflow tweak in my branch but can’t land it to release branch until CI is green… catch-22.

Sahana Iyer: we can cherry-pick the workflow change directly onto the release branch (workflows are allowed). it’ll trigger a new run.

Connor O'Brien: do it. minimal diff only.

Devon Price: ok cherry-picking now.

Devon Price: pushed commit to `runtime-1.18-lts`: `ci: add llvm apt repo for clang-17 on hosted runners`.

CI Bot: Workflow run started: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Rafael Mendes: updated `release` environment with `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` and allowed branch pattern `runtime-*-lts`. should be available on next run.

Sahana Iyer: (reaction: :pray:) watching logs.

Sahana Iyer: toolchain step now passes. clang installs.

Sahana Iyer: publish step is now using the PAT (no warning) and push succeeded:
```
Pushing checksums to runtime-artifacts...
Enumerating objects: 14, done.
To https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts
   8a21c1d..c3f11a0  lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1 -> lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1
```

Allison Grant: nice. does it also upload the provenance json?

Sahana Iyer: yep, artifact list includes:
- `runtime-1.18.1-rc1+g<sha>.tar.zst`
- `checksums.txt`
- `provenance.json`
- `build-manifest.txt`

Sean Gallagher: can we add a note to runbook: release branches require `release` env secret setup + llvm repo, until container pin lands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, I’ll update the runbook + we should file a follow-up to move release branches to toolchain container so we don’t depend on apt repos.

Devon Price: I’ll open a ticket and link here. also, we should flip the workflow so it fails fast if `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` missing (no silent fallback).

Connor O'Brien: agreed.

CI Bot: Workflow run succeeded: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Connor O'Brien: thanks all. action items:
- [Devon] follow-up PR: use toolchain container for release branches; remove apt install.
- [Rafael] confirm env secret restrictions are correct and documented.
- [Sahana] add guardrail: fail if token missing; update checklist/runbook.

Connor O'Brien: also reran the original failed run and marked as obsolete in the thread. :thumbsup:
text:
Connor O'Brien: seeing a weird one: release branch CI failing but main is green. Branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` (cut yesterday). Failing job: `release-ci / build-runtime-images`.

Connor O'Brien: link: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/actions/runs/9142218832

CI Bot: Workflow run failed: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Sahana Iyer: pulled logs. it’s dying during toolchain bootstrap + then again when trying to upload artifacts.

Sahana Iyer: excerpt:
```
==> Step: Setup toolchain
+ ./ci/install_toolchain.sh
Detected branch: runtime-1.18-lts
Using toolchain manifest: ci/manifests/toolchain-release.yaml
Installing clang-17...
E: Unable to locate package clang-17

==> Step: Build
ninja: error: loading 'build.ninja': No such file or directory

==> Step: Publish checksums
+ ./ci/publish_checksums.sh
remote: Permission to redwood/runtime-artifacts denied to github-actions[bot].
fatal: unable to access 'https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts/': The requested URL returned error: 403
```

Devon Price: two separate issues then: runner image doesn’t have apt repo for clang-17, AND token perms for publish step.

Sean Gallagher: why is it only on release branch? main uses the same workflow right?

Sahana Iyer: not exactly. main uses `toolchain-main.yaml` + self-hosted runner group `perf-ci-large`. release branches use `ubuntu-22.04` hosted runners + `toolchain-release.yaml` to keep it “stable”.

Connor O'Brien: ok that explains clang-17 missing. release manifest probably points at 17 but runner’s apt sources don’t have llvm repo configured.

Kira Volkov: also the 403: on release branches we tightened default permissions last week (contents: read). publish script pushes to `runtime-artifacts` repo, so it needs a PAT or workflow permission with `contents: write` + access to that repo.

Rafael Mendes: (reaction: :eyes:) do we have secrets available on release branches? sometimes GH blocks org secrets on non-default branches depending on env rules.

Sahana Iyer: yep, looks like `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` is empty in that run. log line:
```
WARNING: RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN not set; falling back to GITHUB_TOKEN
```
then 403.

Devon Price: I think the secret is scoped to `main` environment only. release workflow uses `environment: release` but the env doesn’t include that secret.

Connor O'Brien: so fix should be:
1) pin toolchain install to our prebuilt toolchain container (no apt)
2) move artifact publish to use `release` environment with the PAT + allow it on release branches

Allison Grant: quick check: this is blocking perf gate artifacts too? if publish fails we won’t get perf-canary summary attached.

Sahana Iyer: yep, `publish_checksums.sh` is in the same job right before it uploads the build manifest + checksums.

Sean Gallagher: for immediate unblock, can we rerun with manual override / skip publish?

Connor O'Brien: would rather not skip; enterprises will expect checksums/provenance even for RCs. but we can get a green build first to validate branch.

Devon Price: I can push a workflow hotfix: add llvm apt repo step for release branches as a short-term patch, then follow up w/ container pin.

Sahana Iyer: +1 short term. add:
```
- name: Add LLVM apt repo
  run: |
    wget -O - https://apt.llvm.org/llvm-snapshot.gpg.key | sudo apt-key add -
    sudo add-apt-repository "deb http://apt.llvm.org/jammy/ llvm-toolchain-jammy-17 main"
    sudo apt-get update
```
then `apt-get install clang-17` should work.

Kira Volkov: re token: we should not rely on `GITHUB_TOKEN` cross-repo. safest is to use `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` PAT with least privilege on that repo.

Rafael Mendes: can we confirm why secret not available? GH settings: env `release` requires approval? could be pending.

Sahana Iyer: checked run summary: no “Waiting for approval” banner. It’s just not present. likely never added to `release` env.

Connor O'Brien: @Sahana can you add the token to `release` environment + restrict to `runtime-*-lts` branches?

Sahana Iyer: I don’t have admin perms for env secrets. we need someone from Infra or repo admin.

Devon Price: I can ping Infra (INT-3302 was about release branch secrets, might still be open). @Rafael can you help?

Rafael Mendes: yep, I can update env secrets. what’s the secret name exactly?

Connor O'Brien: `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` (PAT for https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts). Also `COSIGN_KEY_RELEASE` later but not in this job yet.

Rafael Mendes: ok.

CI Bot: Re-run requested by Connor O'Brien (attempt #2)

Devon Price: I pushed a tiny workflow tweak in my branch but can’t land it to release branch until CI is green… catch-22.

Sahana Iyer: we can cherry-pick the workflow change directly onto the release branch (workflows are allowed). it’ll trigger a new run.

Connor O'Brien: do it. minimal diff only.

Devon Price: ok cherry-picking now.

Devon Price: pushed commit to `runtime-1.18-lts`: `ci: add llvm apt repo for clang-17 on hosted runners`.

CI Bot: Workflow run started: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Rafael Mendes: updated `release` environment with `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` and allowed branch pattern `runtime-*-lts`. should be available on next run.

Sahana Iyer: (reaction: :pray:) watching logs.

Sahana Iyer: toolchain step now passes. clang installs.

Sahana Iyer: publish step is now using the PAT (no warning) and push succeeded:
```
Pushing checksums to runtime-artifacts...
Enumerating objects: 14, done.
To https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts
   8a21c1d..c3f11a0  lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1 -> lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1
```

Allison Grant: nice. does it also upload the provenance json?

Sahana Iyer: yep, artifact list includes:
- `runtime-1.18.1-rc1+g<sha>.tar.zst`
- `checksums.txt`
- `provenance.json`
- `build-manifest.txt`

Sean Gallagher: can we add a note to runbook: release branches require `release` env secret setup + llvm repo, until container pin lands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, I’ll update the runbook + we should file a follow-up to move release branches to toolchain container so we don’t depend on apt repos.

Devon Price: I’ll open a ticket and link here. also, we should flip the workflow so it fails fast if `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` missing (no silent fallback).

Connor O'Brien: agreed.

CI Bot: Workflow run succeeded: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Connor O'Brien: thanks all. action items:
- [Devon] follow-up PR: use toolchain container for release branches; remove apt install.
- [Rafael] confirm env secret restrictions are correct and documented.
- [Sahana] add guardrail: fail if token missing; update checklist/runbook.

Connor O'Brien: also reran the original failed run and marked as obsolete in the thread. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-releases: 1741001123
preview:
Connor O'Brien: We need to land on LTS branch/tag naming today-ish. :grimacing: Options being tossed around: `runtime-1.18-lts` branch vs `release/1.18` branch, and tag formats like `1.18.1-lts` vs `v1.18.1+lts`. Main constraint: Private installer + Helm pinning semantics. Anyone have strong opinions / gotchas?

Sahana Iyer: From CI side: I’d prefer a predictable branch glob we can target in workflows. `runtime-*-lts` is easy. `release/*` is already used by some legacy jobs and I’m worried we’ll accidentally pick up non-LTS release branches.

Bruno Silva: Private installer: we currently parse runtime version as semver in a couple places (upgrade plan + “is upgrade allowed” checks). Suffix `-lts` is technically semver prerelease, which sorts *lower* than the base version.
Bruno Silva: Example: `1.18.1-lts` < `1.18.1` per semver rules. That might be fine, but we need to make sure we never compare LTS tags against non-LTS tags and conclude “downgrade”.

Noah Patel: +1 on not mixing comparisons. Also “+lts” is semver build metadata; semver says build metadata should be ignored for precedence. That means `1.18.1+lts` compares equal to `1.18.1`, which is worse for upgrade logic.

Vivek Kulkarni: Private packaging POV: customers will ask for “pin to LTS patch 1.18.1”. If we do `+lts` we lose a unique sortable identifier. I’d rather make the LTS bit explicit.

Marissa Cole: From product/docs/customer angle: `1.18.1-lts` is easy to communicate. “+lts” is going to confuse basically everyone.

Elliot Price: Release notes generator wants a stable way to infer “this is LTS” without having to maintain a manual list. Suffix is easiest. If it’s in the tag, it’s in the URL and the notes can show “LTS Patch”.

Rafael Mendes: SRE question: how do we prevent folks from accidentally deploying LTS into Hosted where we want mainline? (I know it’s mostly config, but naming helps.) I like branch clearly being LTS-specific.

Connor O'Brien: So seems like `+lts` is out. Remaining debate: branch name and tag prefix.

Sahana Iyer: For branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` is explicit and matches the repo name. Also we can protect it and require approvals.

Bruno Silva: Can we do git tag `runtime-1.18.1-lts` vs `v1.18.1-lts`? For the installer we care more about the runtime *image tag* than the git tag, but we do surface git tags in some audit output.

Noah Patel: `v` prefix is conventional for git tags. But image tags usually don’t include `v`.

Vivek Kulkarni: Installer expectation today:
- image tag like `redwood-runtime:1.18.1` (string, but semver-ish)
- chart values sometimes reference it directly
If we go with `1.18.1-lts` as image tag, that’s fine as a string. The parsing is where we need to be careful.

Bruno Silva: Yeah K8s doesn’t care. Our installer comparison logic does. I can adjust to treat `-lts` as a channel suffix and compare base versions separately.

Marissa Cole: How does it show up in Console? If we have a “channel selector” eventually, it can say LTS and show `1.18.1` underneath, but for now most customers will be copy/pasting tags from docs.

Elliot Price: And release notes URL will likely key on full patch identifier. So we want `1.18.1-lts` as the canonical patch string.

Connor O'Brien: One worry: semver prerelease semantics make `1.18.1-lts` look “less stable” than `1.18.1` to tooling.

Sahana Iyer: True, but we’re not using semver precedence for the docker registry or git hosting. It’s mostly our internal comparisons + maybe Helm.

Bruno Silva: Helm chart `appVersion` is semver validated in some tooling (not all). But the image tag is just a string. If we set chart `appVersion: 1.18.1` and image tag `1.18.1-lts`, that might be clean.

Noah Patel: That’s a good separation:
- “Runtime base version” (semver) = `1.18.1`
- “Channel build identifier” (string) = `1.18.1-lts`

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also helps if we want to say “this LTS patch is based on mainline 1.18.1 + backports” without claiming it’s prerelease.

Talia Benson: Jumping in (sorry late). We need something we can write into the ADR and reuse across docs, support playbook, and installer. If we do base version + channel suffix, we should define it explicitly.

Connor O'Brien: Proposal:
- Branch: `runtime-<MAJOR>.<MINOR>-lts` (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`)
- Git tags: `v<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts`)
- Runtime image tags: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`)
- Helm `appVersion`: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>` (no suffix)
- Private installer: pin image tag; compare base semver only; treat `-lts` as a channel indicator
Thoughts?

Bruno Silva: Works for me if we commit to the installer change: never compare `1.18.1-lts` directly as semver. Compare `1.18.1` and then enforce that channel doesn’t change unless user opts in.

Sahana Iyer: CI ok. We can add workflows for branches matching `runtime-*-lts` and tags matching `v*-lts`.

Elliot Price: Release notes generator can detect `-lts` in tag name and switch to LTS template.

Marissa Cole: Customer comms ok. “Install runtime 1.18.1-lts” reads clearly.

Rafael Mendes: For SRE rollouts: can we also publish a “moving” tag like `1.18-lts` that always points to latest patch? Some customers will ask.

Connor O'Brien: I’m hesitant for the first pass. Moving tags are convenient but scary for auditability. Maybe we can add later as an opt-in alias.

Vivek Kulkarni: Agree. Private customers usually want explicit pinning. If we add moving tag later, it should be documented as “not recommended for regulated”.

Talia Benson: Action items:
- Connor to update ENG-4822 / ADR draft with the above naming.
- Bruno to file/track installer parsing update (pinning semantics + channel lock).
- Sahana to update CI branch/tag globs.

Connor O'Brien: Cool. I’ll post summary in #eng-releases and link the ticket. :thumbsup:

Sahana Iyer: :thumbsup:

Bruno Silva: :+1: I’ll start a quick spike on the semver parsing changes and report back.

Elliot Price: Once the tag is final, I’ll update the release notes template to show the canonical patch string exactly (incl `-lts`).

title: #eng-releases: 1740731152
preview:
Connor O'Brien: heads up for next week’s release notes draft — need a decision on wording for Quantization Profile Library + whether we mention it’s behind an allowlist. currently i have:

> New: Opt-in Quantization Profiles (Beta)
> Enable per-route quantization profiles to reduce inference cost while preserving quality and latency. Available for select customers.

thoughts?

Paula Mendes: +1 we should be super explicit it’s opt-in per route and default behavior unchanged. also we should avoid sounding like auto-optimization.

Marissa Cole: Agree. PM ask: do we say “allowlist” or “available to select customers / by request”? allowlist is accurate but tends to trigger a bunch of “how do i get on it” tickets.

Elliot Price: from docs POV we’ve been using “Beta (opt-in, limited availability)” language. “allowlist” is more internal-y.

Alex Martinez: +1. release notes go to a broad audience. “limited availability” + “contact support/account team” is better.

Ethan Park: re: enablement: it’s behind `quant_profile_library_enabled` tenant gate + per-route config field. so limited availability is true. but we should NOT imply it’s generally available in all regions/models.

Connor O'Brien: ok. but we also don’t want people enabling it and then realizing console toggle isn’t visible.

Naomi Feldman: from security/compliance: we should mention RBAC/audit log exists? or at least avoid implying anyone can toggle it without controls.

Paula Mendes: release notes might be too short for that. but we can link to docs which cover RBAC + audit events.

Elliot Price: proposal:

> Beta: Quantization Profile Library (opt-in, limited availability)
> Publish and enable versioned quantization profiles per route. Includes compatibility checks, safe fallback to baseline, and audit logs for changes.
> Learn more: <docs link>

Alex Martinez: “Publish” might confuse customers (they’re not publishing). maybe:

> Beta: Quantization Profile Library (opt-in, limited availability)
> Enable Redwood-provided, versioned quantization profiles per route to reduce cost while meeting latency SLOs. Compatibility checks + safe fallback to baseline included.

Marissa Cole: +1 on “Redwood-provided”. Also want to avoid promising quality preservation — we do guardrails but still could shift outputs. maybe “while monitoring quality” or “with quality guardrails”.

Paula Mendes: yes. we’ve been saying “reduce cost with eval/quality guardrails” not “preserving quality” as a guarantee.

Ethan Park: +1. plus we’ve got the kill switch. maybe not mention kill switch in release notes.

Connor O'Brien: what about the allowlist part:

Option A) “limited availability”
Option B) “available by allowlist”

I lean A.

Alex Martinez: A. “allowlist” reads like “email us” and causes confusion. “limited availability—contact us to enable” is clear enough.

Naomi Feldman: A is fine. Also please don’t say “self-serve toggle” unless it is actually self-serve for everyone.

Elliot Price: we can add a line in docs: “If you don’t see the toggle, your org may not have access yet.”

Marissa Cole: ok final copy suggestion:

> Beta: Quantization Profile Library (opt-in, limited availability)
> Enable Redwood-provided, versioned quantization profiles per route to reduce inference cost with compatibility checks, safe fallback to baseline, and quality guardrails. If you don’t see the toggle in Console, contact Support.

Connor O'Brien: that works. might be slightly long but acceptable.

ReleaseBot: :memo: Release notes draft updated in `release-notes/2025-03-04.md` by @connor.obrien (commit `a8f1c2d`).

Paula Mendes: can we swap “quality guardrails” -> “evaluation guardrails” to be consistent w/ the PRD language?

Marissa Cole: fine by me.

Elliot Price: +1 “evaluation guardrails” is more concrete.

Connor O'Brien: updated:

> Beta: Quantization Profile Library (opt-in, limited availability)
> Enable Redwood-provided, versioned quantization profiles per route to reduce inference cost with compatibility checks, safe fallback to baseline, and evaluation guardrails. If you don’t see the toggle in Console, contact Support.

locking this unless objections by EOD.

Alex Martinez: :thumbsup:

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: (and ty for not saying “allowlist”)

Ethan Park: :thumbsup:

title: #eng-releases: 1740717722
preview:
Connor O'Brien: need final eyes on runtime release notes blurb for cache telemetry rollout. goal: accurate + not scary. also confirm feature flag defaults for 1.18.0.

Connor O'Brien: current draft in PR-18435 has:
- “Adds prefix/KV cache hit-rate, eviction pressure, and wasted prefill metrics (opt-in via flags).”
- “New miss reason breakdown (capacity/ttl_expired/policy_disabled/hash_mismatch/version_mismatch).”
- “Sampling + rate limiting for high-volume cache events.”

Connor O'Brien: questions:
1) default flags in prod? staging? dedicated?
2) do we call out dashboards exist (internal) vs Console (not yet)?
3) do we mention metric name change / deprecations now or later?

Omar El-Sayed: for defaults: i strongly prefer OFF by default in prod for first cut, ON in staging + canary. instrumentation is low overhead but we’re still validating ingestion/cardinality in the wild.

Omar El-Sayed: flags from PR-18429:
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.enabled` (master)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.sample_rate` (float 0..1)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.emit_miss_reasons` (extra labels)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.emit_histograms` (prefix reuse depth + token buckets)

Omar El-Sayed: we can set enabled=true in canary only, sample_rate=0.01 in prod once we turn it on. miss reasons + histograms should be behind their own flags too.

Paula Mendes: from product side: please do NOT say “Console support” yet. we’re only doing internal dashboards + maybe an internal report. wording should set expectation that this is internal observability + rollout guardrails.

Paula Mendes: recommend: “New internal cache efficiency telemetry (behind flags) to support ongoing cost/latency optimization work.”

Marcus Reed: +1 to conservative defaults. perf canary results were clean at 1% sample rate, but we didn’t run at 100% for long. i’d do:
- staging: enabled=true, sample_rate=1.0
- perf-canary/prod-canary: enabled=true, sample_rate=0.25 (or 0.1)
- prod: enabled=false (initial), then enabled=true sample_rate=0.01 after 24h clean canary

Marcus Reed: also pls include a line “no raw prompts logged; metrics are bucketed” (we keep getting questions from sec review).

Allison Grant: for deprecations: we did the rename in PR-18431. dashboards are already updated on observability-pack side, but release notes should mention “old metric names deprecated; see internal metrics guide” so oncall doesn’t get confused if they have local grafana panels.

Allison Grant: maybe a short line: “cache metrics aligned to naming/cardinality policy; previous experimental metric names will stop emitting in a future release.” avoid promising exact removal date.

Nadia Rahman: on “emit_miss_reasons”: be careful. that label set is acceptable only with the route/model allowlist and prompt length bucketing. we should not encourage folks to enable miss reasons in prod without also having ingestion guards deployed.

Nadia Rahman: release notes should point to the rollout runbook / checklist (internal link) + call out that enabling miss reasons increases series count.

Connor O'Brien: ok so proposal:
- defaults in 1.18.0: prod `runtime.cache_telemetry.enabled=false`
- staging: enabled=true sample_rate=1
- canary: enabled=true sample_rate=0.1
- `emit_miss_reasons=false` by default everywhere, `emit_histograms=true` only in staging/canary?

Omar El-Sayed: i’d rather histograms default off too in prod/canary until we confirm bucket sanity. staging yes.

Marcus Reed: agree. histograms are the easiest way to accidentally crank ingestion volume.

Paula Mendes: wording-wise: make it sound like a gradual rollout. also clarify “cache hit-rate” here includes prefix reuse + kv reuse (per ADR) so ppl don’t compare to old definition.

Allison Grant: pls add: “Known limitation: metrics may appear lower during streaming retries; fixed in PR-18428” (or just “retry handling improved”). depends on whether 18428 is in this release.

Connor O'Brien: 18428 is in, yes.

Ava Chen: jumping in — keep the release note minimal + link out. we don’t want customers reading internal release notes and thinking they need to do something. tone: “adds instrumentation; off by default; can be enabled for debugging/analysis.”

Connor O'Brien: got it. updated release note snippet draft:
“Cache telemetry (experimental): Adds new prefix/KV cache efficiency metrics (hit-rate, miss reasons, evictions/pressure, wasted prefill) to support internal observability and optimization work. Off by default in production; enabled in staging/canary per rollout plan. Telemetry is privacy-safe (no raw prompts) and uses bucketing/sampling to control overhead. Note: enabling miss-reason labels and histograms increases metric cardinality; follow the cache telemetry rollout runbook.”

Connor O'Brien: plus short bullet: “Metric names aligned to cache telemetry naming standards; previous experimental names are deprecated and will be removed in a future release.”

Nadia Rahman: that’s good. small tweak: say “miss reason breakdown (optional)” instead of implying it always emits.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also make sure we list the actual config keys somewhere (maybe “see runtime config docs”). folks will ask in incident.

Allison Grant: add a one-liner “See: dashboards → Cache Efficiency / Prefix+KV” (internal) if release notes are internal-facing only.

Paula Mendes: ok if internal release notes. please don’t mention Console.

Connor O'Brien: confirmed these are internal runtime release notes.

DeployBot: buildkite #runtime-main — Release Notes Check: PR-18435 updated by @connor. awaiting approvals.

Connor O'Brien: final: setting defaults:
- prod: `runtime.cache_telemetry.enabled=false`
- prod (when enabled): `sample_rate=0.01`, `emit_miss_reasons=false`, `emit_histograms=false`
- canary: `enabled=true`, `sample_rate=0.1`, others false
- staging: `enabled=true`, `sample_rate=1.0`, `emit_histograms=true`, `emit_miss_reasons=true` (ok?)

Nadia Rahman: staging miss reasons ok. just make sure observability-pack PR-613 (cardinality guards) is already deployed to staging ingestion.

Allison Grant: it is in staging as of yesterday. prod deploy scheduled after canary.

Marcus Reed: defaults look sane. perf canary gate will catch if overhead surprises.

Omar El-Sayed: 👍 works for runtime.

Paula Mendes: 👍

Ava Chen: approve. make sure the runbook link is the current one.

Connor O'Brien: cool, pushing update + tagging for approvals.
